md: ### What is a Monte Carlo simulation?

Some questions are hard to answer with a neat formula, but easy to answer by **trying something at random, many times, and counting what happens**. That is the whole idea of a *Monte Carlo simulation*: use a flood of random samples to estimate an answer.

Scientists first leaned on this trick in the 1940s, for problems far too tangled for ordinary maths. Today it turns up everywhere: finance, weather forecasting, biology, game AI, and computer graphics. (Its playful name has a good story, which we will get to at the end.)
### The idea: estimate π by throwing darts
Picture a square dartboard with a circle drawn inside it, just touching all four sides. Now throw darts at it **completely at random**, so every spot in the square is equally likely to be hit.

Some darts land *inside* the circle; some land in the *corners outside* it. Here is the key insight: the share of darts that land inside the circle should match the share of the **area** the circle takes up.
### Why that gives us π
The circle fits snugly inside the square, so its radius $r$ is exactly half the square's side. That single fact is all we need. Look at the picture:
Random darts spread evenly across the **whole square**, so the share landing inside the circle matches the circle's share of the area: about $0.785$, which is exactly $\pi/4$.
Now turn that around. If the inside-fraction equals $\pi/4$, then multiplying it by $4$ gives $\pi$:

blocks.tex: \pi \approx 4 \times \frac{\text{darts inside the circle}}{\text{darts thrown in total}}

blocks.title: The recipe
blocks.body: Throw random darts. Count how many land inside the circle. Multiply that fraction by $4$. The more darts you throw, the closer the result creeps to the real $\pi = 3.14159\ldots$

md: ### Run the simulation
Time to play. The controls under the dartboard put you in charge:
- **▶ Run** turns on a continuous stream of darts; press it again to pause.
- **+1, +100, +1000** throw an exact batch, so you can step through carefully.
- **↺ Reset** clears the board and starts fresh.
- **speed** sets how many darts fly per frame while running.
Green darts landed inside the circle; rose-coloured darts missed. The chart on the right tracks your π estimate after every batch.

blocks.title: Try this first: throw a single dart
blocks.body:
Press the +1 dart button just once and look at the π estimate. It can only be one of two things: $4$ (if that one dart landed inside the circle) or $0$ (if it missed). Both are wildly far from $3.14159$.
That is the whole reason Monte Carlo needs *big* numbers. One dart tells you almost nothing, the same way flipping a coin once and getting heads does not prove the coin is two-headed. The real answer only steps out of the randomness once you have thrown thousands of darts.

md: ### What to watch for

- **Watch it settle.** With only a few dozen darts the estimate lurches around. Press *Run* and, as thousands pile up, the line on the chart stops jumping and homes in on $3.14159\ldots$ This steady settling has a name: the *law of large numbers*. Random noise averages out.

- **Convergence is slow.** Going from $100$ to $1000$ darts helps a lot, but $1000$ to $10{,}000$ helps less. Each extra correct digit needs roughly **100 times** more darts. Monte Carlo is simple and general, but it is not fast.

blocks.title: Why use a slow method?
blocks.body:
If Monte Carlo is slow, why bother? Because it barely cares how hard the problem is. The same throw-and-count trick estimates the area of *any* shape, the value of *any* awkward integral, or the risk inside a financial model, places where exact formulas may not exist at all.
There is a second reason. Real life usually gives you only *one* run: one weather season, one market, one roll of the dice. A computer can replay thousands of those random runs in seconds and show you the full range of what *could* happen. Estimating $\pi$ is simply the friendliest place to meet the method.

md: ### Where the name comes from

The method has a surprisingly playful name. **Monte Carlo** is a district of Monaco famous for its grand casinos, and the technique borrows that name because, just like a casino, it runs entirely on chance.

The story goes back to the 1940s. A mathematician named Stanislaw Ulam was recovering from an illness and passing the time playing solitaire. He started to wonder: what are the chances a game of solitaire actually works out? Calculating it with pure maths was a nightmare. Then he had a better idea, just *deal the cards many times over and count* how often it works.

He and the famous mathematician John von Neumann soon realised this simple counting trick could crack serious physics problems that no formula could touch. They needed a secret code name for the work, and a colleague suggested *Monte Carlo*, a nod to an uncle of Ulam's who loved to gamble there.

And here is the lovely twist. A casino uses the law of large numbers to slowly and reliably take money from its customers. A Monte Carlo simulation uses the *very same* law to slowly and reliably pull the truth out of randomness. Same mathematics, opposite purpose.

blocks.title: 🤖 The bigger picture: this same idea powers AI
blocks.body:
You just used the **big idea** behind a huge slice of modern computing: when a problem is too tangled for a neat formula, do not solve it, *sample* it. Run many random trials and let the data hand you the answer.
Machine learning runs on the very same instinct, just pointed the other way. Monte Carlo already **knows the rules** (here, the circle equation) and simulates *forward* to estimate a number. Machine learning starts with only **examples** (say, photos labelled cat or dog) and works *backward* to discover the rule itself.
Better still, the two team up. Monte Carlo is one of the engines humming inside today's AI:
- training a neural network feeds it **random batches** of data,
- the AlphaGo program beat world champions using **Monte Carlo Tree Search**, playing out thousands of random futures,
- AI image generators build pictures by **sampling random noise**, over and over.
Throw enough random darts, in other words, and you can estimate π, recognise a face, or master a board game.
